concepts.title: Angular velocity in circular motion
concepts.conceptBody:
Angular velocity ($\omega )$

When a particle moves along a circle, it covers some arc length $\left( =\text{s} \right)$ along the circumference in time $\text{t}$ sec. The angle subtended by this arc at the centre or the angle rotated by the radius vector is known as angular displacement in time $t$ sec.

If the particle goes from $A$ to $B$

angular displacement $=\angle \text{AOB}=\theta $

distance covered $=\text{arcAB}=\text{s}$

By definition of $\theta $ in radian,

$\theta =\frac{\text{s}}{\text{r}}$

The angular displacement per unit time is known as angular velocity.

$\omega =\frac{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ angular }\!\!~\!\!\text{ displacement }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ time }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}$

$\omega =\frac{\theta }{\text{t}}=\frac{\text{s}/\text{r}}{\text{t}}=\frac{1}{\text{r}}\left( \frac{\text{s}}{\text{r}} \right)$

$\text{now }\!\!~\!\!\text{ s/t }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }=\text{v}=\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ speed }\!\!~\!\!\text{ of }\!\!~\!\!\text{ the }\!\!~\!\!\text{ particle}\text{.}$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\omega =\frac{\text{v}}{\text{r}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ v}=\text{r}\omega $

concepts.title: Time Period of Revolution
concepts.conceptBody:
Time period of revolution (T) 

If the particle completes one revolution, $\theta =2\pi $

Let time for one revolution $=\text{T}$,

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\omega =\frac{2\pi }{\text{T}}$

Let $\text{n}=$ frequency of revolution

$=$ number of revolution completed in one second

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ n}=\frac{1}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ T}}=\frac{\omega }{2\pi }$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\omega =\frac{2\pi }{\text{T}}=2\pi \text{n}$ and

$\text{v}=\text{r}\omega =\frac{2\pi \text{r}}{\text{T}}=2\pi \text{rn}$

concepts.title: Velocity and acceleration in circular motion
concepts.conceptBody:
Velocity and acceleration in circular motion

The magnitude of velocity does not change with time but the direction of the velocity (along tangent) keeps on changing from moment to moment. As the velocity vector changes direction with time, the acceleration is non-zero in uniform circular motion.

Let $\text{v}=$ speed of moving particle (same at all points) and $\text{r}=$ radius of the circle.

Let the particle start from $\text{A}$ at $\text{t}=0$. After time $\text{t}$, it reaches point $\text{P}$ where the position vector is :

$\bar{r}\left( \text{t} \right)=\overline{\text{OP}}=\text{rcos}\omega \text{t}\overset{}{\mathop{\text{i}}}\,+\text{rsin}\omega \text{t}$j

$\Rightarrow \!\!~\!\!\bar{v}\left( t \right)=\frac{d\bar{r}}{dt}=-r\omega \text{sin}\omega t\,\hat{i}+r\omega \text{cos}\omega t\,\hat{j}$

acceleration \(=\bar{a}\left( t \right)=\frac{d\bar{v}}{dt}=r{{\omega }^{2}}\text{cos}\omega t\,\hat{i}-r{{\omega }^{2}}\text{sin}\omega {{t}^{2}}\,\hat{j}\)

$=-{{\omega }^{2}}\left( r\text{cos}\omega t\,\hat{i}-r{{\omega }^{2}}\text{sin}\omega t\,\hat{j} \right)$

$\Rightarrow \bar{a}=-{{\omega }^{2}}\bar{r}$

The magnitude of the acceleration is ${{\omega }^{2}}\text{r}$ and it is directed towards the centre $\text{O}$.

As the acceleration is directed toward the centre, it is known as centripetal acceleration or radial acceleration (along the radius).

$\Rightarrow \!\!~\!\!$ centripetal acceleration $={{\omega }^{2}}\text{r}={{\text{v}}^{2}}/\text{r}$

NOTE: So far we have observed that in uniform circular motion, the magnitude of velocity (v) and magnitude of acceleration $({{\text{v}}^{2}}/\text{r})$ are constant, while the direction of the velocity (along the tangent) and the direction of acceleration (along the radius) keep on changing with time.

concepts.title: Non Uniform Circular Motion
concepts.conceptBody:
Non-uniform Circular Motion

If the speed of the particle rotating in the circle changes with time, it is said to be in non-uniform circular motion . The acceleration of the particle in that case has two components:

(1) A radical (or centripetal) component which causes the changes in the direction of the velocity. It is directed towards the centre and has a magnitude ${{a}_{r}}$ given as : $ar={{v}^{2}}/r$

Note that this component is also present in uniform circular motion.

(2) A tangential component which causes the change in magnitude of velocity. It is directed along the tangent and its magnitude is decided by the net tangential force acting on the particle. Its magnitude is given by ${{\text{a}}_{\text{t}}}$ as:

${{a}_{t}}=\frac{dv}{dt}$ where $v$ is the speed of the particle.

The tangential acceleration is in the direction of motion if the particle speeds up and opposite to the direction of motion if the particle slows down.

NOTE: In uniform circular motion, tangential component ${{\text{a}}_{\text{t}}}=0\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ m}/{{\text{s}}^{2}}$ because speed does not change.

concepts.title: Equations of Circular Motion
concepts.conceptBody:
Equations of Circular Motion

Circular motion is a special case of curvillinear motion where radius of curvature is same at all points of trajectory.

${{\text{a}}_{\text{t}}}=\left( \frac{\text{dv}}{\text{dt}} \right),{{\text{a}}_{\text{r}}}=\left( \frac{{{\text{V}}^{2}}}{\text{r}} \right) {{\text{a}}_{\text{t}}}=\text{ tangential accderation}$

$\overrightarrow{\text{r}}=(\text{R}\cos \theta )\hat{\text{i}}+(\text{R}\sin \theta )\hat{\text{j}} {{\text{a}}_{\text{r}}}=\text{ radial acceleration}$

$\overrightarrow{\text{V}}=\left( \frac{\text{d}\overrightarrow{\text{r}}}{\text{dt}} \right)$

Also, $(\overrightarrow{\text{V}}\cdot \overrightarrow{\text{R}}=0)\Rightarrow (\overrightarrow{\text{V}}\bot \overrightarrow{\text{R}}) \vec{r}=(R\cos \theta )i+(R\sin \theta )j$

$\overrightarrow{\text{V}}=\frac{\text{d}\overrightarrow{\text{r}}}{\text{dt}}=\text{R}(-\sin \theta )\left( \frac{\text{d}\theta }{\text{dt}} \right)\hat{\text{i}}+\text{R}\cos \theta \left( \frac{\text{d}\theta }{\text{dt}} \right)\hat{\text{j}},\quad \left\{ \because \frac{\text{d}\theta }{\text{dt}}=\text{w} \right\}$

$=\bar{v}\operatorname{Rw}(-\sin \theta )\hat{i}+\operatorname{Rw}(\cos \theta )\hat{j}$

$=\vec{a}\left( \frac{d\hat{v}}{dt} \right)=\frac{d}{dt}(\omega R[-\sin \theta \hat{i}+\cos \theta \hat{j}])$

$=R(-\sin \theta i+\cos \theta j)\frac{d\omega }{dt}+(R\omega )(-\cos \theta i-\sin \theta j)\frac{d\omega }{dt}$

$=\alpha R(-\sin \theta i+\cos \theta j)+{{\omega }^{2}}R(-\cos \theta i-\sin \theta j)$

$=\alpha R\left( {{{\hat{u}}}_{\theta }} \right)+{{w}^{2}}R\left( -{{{\hat{u}}}_{r}} \right)$

$\left\{ \because \alpha =\frac{d\omega }{dt} \right\}$

Let the unit vector along tangent and radius be

${{\hat{u}}_{\theta }}=\vec{V}=\left( -\text{sin}\theta \right)\hat{i}+\left( \text{cos}\theta \right)\hat{j}$

${{\hat{u}}_{\text{r}}}=\frac{{\vec{r}}}{\left| {\vec{r}} \right|}=\left( \text{cos}\theta \right)\text{i}+\left( \text{sin}\theta \right)\text{j}$

$\vec{v}=\omega R\left( {{{\hat{u}}}_{\theta }} \right)$

$\vec{a}=\alpha \text{R}\left( {{{\hat{u}}}_{\theta }} \right)+{{\omega }^{2}}\text{R}\left( -{{{\hat{u}}}_{\text{r}}} \right)$

$\vec{a}=\left( \vec{a}+{{{\vec{a}}}_{\text{r}}} \right)$

${{\text{a}}_{\text{t}}}=\left( \alpha \text{R} \right)=\frac{\text{d}}{\text{dt}}\left( \omega \text{R} \right)=\left( \frac{\text{dv}}{\text{dt}} \right)$

${{\text{a}}_{\text{t}}}=\left( \frac{\text{dv}}{\text{dt}} \right)=\left( \frac{\text{vdv}}{\text{ds}} \right)$

${{\text{a}}_{\text{t}}}=\alpha \text{R}=\frac{\text{Rd}\omega }{\text{dt}}=\text{R}\left( \frac{\omega \text{d}\omega }{\text{d}\theta } \right)$

${{\text{a}}_{\text{r}}}=\omega \text{R}=\frac{{{\text{v}}^{2}}}{\text{r}}$

$$ generally angle b/w net acceleration is asked.

Rotational eqns. can be derived as

$\omega ={{\omega }_{0}}+\alpha t \omega =\left( \frac{dv}{dt} \right)$ … (1)

$\theta ={{\omega }_{0}}t+\frac{1}{2}\alpha {{t}^{2}} \alpha =\left( \frac{d\omega }{dt} \right)$ … (2)

${{\omega }^{2}}=\omega _{0}^{2}+2\alpha \theta  \alpha =\left( \omega \frac{d\omega }{d\theta } \right)$ … (3)

In vector form, we have following relations:

(1) $\vec{V}=\vec{\omega }\times \vec{r}$

(2) ${{\vec{a}}_{\text{r}}}=-{{(\omega )}^{2}}\vec{r}=\left( \vec{\omega }\times \vec{v} \right)$

(3) ${{\vec{a}}_{t}}=\left( \vec{\alpha }\times \vec{r} \right)$
